# Questions in Livestream 17

Live Stream #17: Back to Prime Time

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35</a> | Did the Egyptians have a rich music culture, and what instruments did they prefer? | Yes. He highlighted flutes, sistra, cymbals, and large harp-like instruments as especially important. | He says music was important in temple ritual and in Egyptian culture more broadly. The examples he gives are concrete instruments rather than a reconstructed sound: papyrus flutes, finger cymbals, the religiously important sistrum, and large lyre or harp-like instruments shown with musicians and dancers in tomb scenes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:50</a> | Who might the historical Nimrod have been? | He said he had not studied the question enough to decide, though Sargon and Naram-Sin are common candidates. | He does not endorse an identification. He says he has not examined the question in enough detail and only notes that Sargon and Naram-Sin are among the usual proposals, so the answer remains deliberately tentative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:35</a> | What were the Urim and Thummim, and where did the temple candelabrum come from? | He tentatively favored Heiser's dice-based theory for the Urim and Thummim and said the menorah's deeper antecedents remain uncertain. | He treats the Urim and Thummim as a divination device and says Michael Heiser's proposal that they functioned like dice is his preferred explanation, though not with total certainty. On the candelabrum, he does not claim a clear source and leaves the deeper background of the menorah unresolved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:56</a> | If cherubim and seraphim use imagery connected to Egyptian gods, what does that say theologically about Yahweh? | It emphasizes Yahweh's absolute holiness and uniqueness, because the symbolism is redirected toward a God who needs no idol or human care. | He argues that shared ancient imagery does not lower Yahweh into the same category as Egyptian gods. Instead, the imagery is redirected to emphasize holiness and divine otherness: Yahweh is enthroned with heavenly beings but is not embodied in an idol, fed, washed, or maintained by human ritual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=948" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:48</a> | How do you interpret God hardening Pharaoh's heart in an Egyptian context? Did God take away Pharaoh's free will, and where can someone read more? | He said Exodus first shows Pharaoh hardening his own heart, then God confirms him in the path he freely chose. He did not name a specific reading source. | His answer preserves Pharaoh's agency: Pharaoh initially resists on his own, and God's later hardening confirms him in that chosen rebellion rather than making him innocent. He does not provide a reading recommendation in the answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=1106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:26</a> | Who were the Nephilim and the Rephaim? | He leaned toward viewing the Nephilim as the offspring of a fallen priestly institution, but said he needed more work before speaking confidently about the Rephaim. | He offers a provisional view of the Nephilim, tying them to a corrupt or fallen priestly line rather than presenting a fully settled answer. He is much less confident on the Rephaim and says he needs more study before giving a firm explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=1335" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:15</a> | Has Jeremiah 19:6 about the Valley of Hinnom been fulfilled already, and is it about hell? | He said Jeremiah is speaking first about the literal valley and its history, with the later hell imagery arising as a later idealization. | He reads Jeremiah in its immediate historical setting, as a prophecy about the physical Valley of Hinnom and its association with judgment. Later Jewish and Christian use of Gehenna develops that valley into hell imagery, but he does not make Jeremiah 19 only or first about final hell. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=1655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:35</a> | Why are some cherubim in Ezekiel and Revelation described as having three animal heads and one human head, and where do the ophanim come from? | He treated the animal imagery as analogical language for spiritual beings and, after clarification, connected the wheels with eyes to God's portable throne imagery. | He cautions that the animal faces are symbolic or analogical descriptions of spiritual beings, not a zoological account. When the question turns to the ophanim, he connects the wheels full of eyes to the vision of God's mobile throne. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=2168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:08</a> | Do the priestly garments in Leviticus come from somewhere in the ancient Near East? | Some elements do, but he said the full high-priestly outfit does not map neatly onto Egyptian parallels and may reflect a more distinct synthesis. | He acknowledges that individual priestly items have ancient Near Eastern or Egyptian analogues, but he resists reducing the whole outfit to one source. The high-priestly ensemble in Leviticus appears to combine recognizable elements in a distinctive Israelite form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=2425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:25</a> | Does Egyptian connect breath, wind, and spirit the way Hebrew and Greek do? | Only partially. Breath is tied to life, but Egyptian divides personal spiritual functions differently through the ka and ba. | He says Egyptian can connect breath with life, but it does not map cleanly onto Hebrew ruach or Greek pneuma. Egyptian anthropology distributes spiritual or personal functions through categories such as the ka and ba, so the overlap is partial rather than exact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=2631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:51</a> | Why would copyists of the Old Testament leave numbers unchanged after their cultural context became distant? | He said reverence for the received text usually kept scribes from updating numbers or place names, though some changes did occur and can complicate interpretation. | He explains the stability as a scribal and religious instinct to preserve inherited wording instead of modernizing it. That does not mean every detail was never altered, but the normal posture was to transmit the text as received even when later readers no longer shared the original cultural assumptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=3029" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:29</a> | What books would he recommend on Arabian polytheism or Amorite beliefs? | He said the literature exists but lies outside his specialty, so he could only point generally toward work on old Arabian history and Mesopotamian sources. | He declines to give a confident bibliography because the topic sits outside his main field. His direction is only broad: look to old Arabian historical work for Arabian polytheism and to Mesopotamian material for Amorite beliefs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=3290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:50</a> | Do the huge reign lengths in Sumerian and Babylonian king lists show that humans really lived hundreds of thousands of years ago? | No. He treated those figures as idiomatic or schematic rather than literal chronological records. | He rejects a literal reading of the enormous reign lengths. The figures are better understood as rhetorical, schematic, or traditional ways of portraying remote antiquity and royal legitimacy, not as evidence that individual humans reigned for hundreds of thousands of years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=3683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:23</a> | Could the Tempest Stela refer to the Thera eruption? | Possibly, but he said the dating problems around Thera are still too unresolved to be confident. | He leaves the identification open but cautious. The Tempest Stela could be discussed in relation to the Thera eruption, yet the chronological problems around the eruption and Egyptian dating keep him from treating the connection as settled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=3818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:38</a> | Why does he dislike archaeoastronomy so much? | Because the observations are too unstable, too assumption-heavy, and often ask the evidence to do jobs it was never meant to do. | His objection is methodological. He thinks archaeoastronomical claims often depend on uncertain alignments, assumptions about what ancient builders intended, and evidence that is too flexible to support the strong conclusions people draw from it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=4328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:08</a> | What was the nature of the spirit breathed in for the Egyptians? | He clarified that breath was life for Egyptians, while personal spiritual faculties were divided between the ka and ba rather than identified with breath itself. | He distinguishes breath from a full theory of the soul. In the Egyptian material he describes, breath gives or marks life, while categories such as ka and ba carry the personal and spiritual functions that a modern reader might be tempted to collapse into "spirit." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=4466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:26</a> | Does Genesis 13:10's comparison to "the land of Egypt" reflect an Exodus perspective? | Yes. He said it likely reflects a later Exodus-aware frame rather than a neutral geographical aside. | He reads the comparison as more than a generic geographical marker. The wording assumes an audience for whom Egypt and the Exodus-shaped memory of Egypt are meaningful, so he takes it as an Exodus-aware framing of the scene. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=4745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:05</a> | Who is his least favorite ruler of Egypt? | Hatshepsut. He described her as an opportunistic usurper and gave a strongly negative character reading of her reign. | He names Hatshepsut and gives a sharply negative evaluation, focusing on her seizure and presentation of royal power. The answer is framed as his personal least-favorite choice rather than a neutral ranking of historical significance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=5033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:53</a> | Is the tree in Eden related to Asherah, or is it Mesopotamian? | He said the Eden tree belongs to a broader ancient Near Eastern tree symbolism and that Asherah is more closely comparable to an Osiris-style cult object. | He places the Eden tree within wider ancient Near Eastern sacred-tree symbolism instead of assigning it narrowly to Asherah or Mesopotamia alone. His comparison treats Asherah more as a cultic object with parallels to Osiris-style symbolism than as the direct source of Eden's tree. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=5376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:36</a> | How common was it for generals like Horemheb to become ruler of Egypt? | Not common, but not unique either. He stressed that Egyptian kingship was fundamentally military in character. | He says a general becoming pharaoh was unusual enough to notice but not without precedent. The broader reason it could happen is that Egyptian kingship always had a strong military dimension, so successful commanders could become plausible royal figures in the right circumstances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVpJZyvwIq8?t=5603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:23</a> | How should someone balance knowledge and spirituality without leaning too much on their own understanding? | He rejected the split between them and said scholarship can itself be an act of worship when kept humble under God's word. | He argues that knowledge and spirituality should not be treated as competing goods. Study becomes spiritually healthy when it is practiced humbly, under God's authority, and as part of worship rather than as self-reliant pride. |
